food_name: Chap Chye (Nonya Braised Mixed Vegetables with Glass Noodles)
aliases: Chap Chye (Nonya Braised Mixed Vegetables with Glass Noodles)

english: Chap Chye (Nonya Braised Mixed Vegetables with Glass Noodles)

region_of_origin: Singapore and Malacca, Straits Chinese Peranakan heritage
food_subgroup: Slow-braised mixed vegetable dish with glass noodles (tang hoon) — a complex medley of napa cabbage, wood ear fungus, tofu skin, lily buds, dried shrimp, and glass noodles braised in tau cheo (fermented soybean paste)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Chap Chye. Closest parent is braised mixed vegetable dish (FOODON:03315763 — mixed vegetable dish). No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Mixed vegetable dishes — cooked composite vegetable dish; closest FoodEx2 parent for braised mixed vegetable preparations
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Chap Chye or Nonya braised mixed vegetables. A00LC (mixed vegetable dishes) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Chap Chye or Nonya braised mixed vegetable dish.

main_ingredients: glass noodles (tang hoon, mung bean starch)

serving.description: 1 side dish portion (shared dish at table with rice and other dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side dish serving of Chap Chye is approximately 250g, representing one portion from a shared dish at a Peranakan or Singaporean meal table alongside rice and other dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chap Chye — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient profile with USDA SR Legacy component derivation for dried and specialty ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Foodo.sg + USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Chap Chye as a composite dish. Micronutrient values for specialty dried ingredients (wood ear fungus, lily buds, tofu skin, dried shrimp) cross-referenced and supplemented using USDA SR Legacy component data. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mung bean glass noodles (cellophane noodles / tang hoon) and composite vegetable dish estimation — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chap Chye as a complete dish. GI of 42 is estimated using glass noodles (mung bean starch, cellophane noodles) as the primary carbohydrate contributor, which has a published GI of approximately 45 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). The high dietary fibre content (2.8g/100g) from napa cabbage, wood ear fungus, and lily buds further attenuates postprandial glucose response, supporting a composite dish GI estimate of ~42. GL per 250g serving = (42 × 7.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 7.9, classified as low.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from tau cheo (fermented soybean paste) and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: A 250g serving of Chap Chye delivers approximately 950mg sodium (47% of the WHO 2,000mg daily limit), primarily from tau cheo and dried shrimp; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150g or request a reduced-sodium preparation, and pair with low-sodium dishes to stay within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~42) driven by mung bean glass noodles and high dietary fibre
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chap Chye has a low GI of approximately 42 and a glycemic load of only 7.9 per 250g serving, making it one of the most diabetes-friendly Nonya dishes; the high dietary fibre (2.8g/100g) from napa cabbage, wood ear fungus, and lily buds further slows glucose absorption, and the moderate protein from tofu skin and dried shrimp supports satiety without spiking postprandial glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and anti-inflammatory plant compounds; sodium is the primary cardiovascular concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chap Chye is low in saturated fat (0.8g/100g) and contains no trans fat, with dietary fibre (2.8g/100g) supporting LDL cholesterol reduction; however, the sodium content (~380mg/100g) warrants portion control for individuals managing cardiovascular risk, and the isoflavones from tofu skin and beta-glucans from wood ear fungus provide additional cardioprotective benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from wood ear fungus, tofu skin isoflavones, and high vegetable micronutrient content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Wood ear fungus provides beta-glucan polysaccharides with immunomodulatory properties, tofu skin contributes isoflavones (genistein, daidzein) with documented anti-inflammatory effects, and the high vitamin C content (8mg/100g) enhances non-haem iron absorption; the dish is low in proinflammatory saturated fat, making it a beneficial choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chap Chye (杂菜, meaning 'mixed vegetables' in Hokkien/Teochew) is a slow-braised vegetable medley that is a cornerstone of the Peranakan Chinese New Year reunion dinner table in Singapore and Malacca. Despite its vegetable-dominant appearance, the dish achieves remarkable depth of flavour and nutritional complexity through a wide variety of dried and fresh ingredients: rehydrated wood ear fungus (black and crunchy, contributing beta-glucan polysaccharides), dried lily buds or golden needles (slightly tart and fibrous), tofu skin strips (creamy and protein-rich), glass noodles made from mung bean starch (slippery, absorbent, and low-GI), dried shrimp (for umami and selenium), napa cabbage, carrots, and baby corn. The sauce base is tau cheo (fermented soybean paste) fried with garlic and shallots, which gives the dish its characteristic savoury, slightly funky depth. The entire preparation is slow-braised until all components are tender and the flavours meld into a complex, deeply savoury braise. Nutritionally, Chap Chye is one of the most favourable Nonya dishes: it provides 72 kcal per 100g, 4.8g protein, 2.8g dietary fibre, and a low glycemic index of approximately 42 driven by the mung bean glass noodles. The primary clinical concern is sodium (~380mg/100g, ~950mg per 250g serving) from tau cheo and dried shrimp, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and rich in plant micronutrients including folate, vitamin C, iron, calcium, and potassium.
common_user_queries: chap chye Chinese New Year dish
meal_context: Peranakan Chinese New Year reunion dinner
shared side dish alongside rice and other dishes

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved source for Singapore dishes) with USDA SR Legacy cross-reference for specialty dried ingredients. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (mung bean glass noodles, GI ~45) with documented estimation basis. All mandatory multilingual names present. All hard gates pass.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chap Chye as a composite dish is not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite braised dish is not reported in the primary source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). GI of 42 is an analogous estimate based on mung bean glass noodles (GI ~45, University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary carbohydrate contributor, adjusted downward slightly for the high dietary fibre matrix of the composite dish. Sodium content (~380mg/100g) is the primary clinical concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. A 250g serving delivers approximately 950mg sodium. The dish contains dried shrimp and is therefore not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals. Tau cheo contains soy and may contain gluten depending on preparation. FoodEx2 code A00LC assigned as closest defensible parent (mixed vegetable dishes); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Chap Chye.
